docs.title: About This Site
docs.text: This site is built with Material for MkDocs and documents my learning experiences, code examples, and insights as I develop my Python skills.

docs.title: Adding Resilience with Tenacity: Retry Logic for HTTP Requests
docs.text:
After building a simple, testable weather API client, the next logical step was making it resilient to transient failures.

Network requests fail. Servers time out. APIs rate-limit. These aren't bugs—they're expected conditions that production code needs to handle gracefully.

This post explains how I added retry logic with exponential backoff using the tenacity library, and what I learned about designing fault-tolerant systems.

docs.title: The problem: network requests are unreliable
docs.text:
My initial api_call() function was simple:

def api_call(session, url, params=None, timeout_s=3.0):
    response = session.get(url, params=params, timeout=timeout_s)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response

This works perfectly—until it doesn't.

What happens when: - The network hiccups? - The API returns a 503 "Service Unavailable"? - The server is temporarily overloaded?

The function would fail immediately, even though retrying a second later might succeed.

docs.title: Why not just wrap it in a loop?
docs.text:
I could have written manual retry logic:

for attempt in range(3):
    try:
        return session.get(...)
    except Exception:
        if attempt == 2:
            raise
        time.sleep(2 ** attempt)  # exponential backoff

But this approach has problems: - Logic mixed with behaviour (retry config embedded in code) - Hard to test (retry timing is hardcoded) - Not reusable (every function needs its own retry loop) - Error-prone (easy to get backoff math wrong)

Instead, I used tenacity—a library designed specifically for this.

docs.title: Introducing tenacity
docs.text:
tenacity is a Python library that provides declarative retry logic through decorators.

The key insight: retries are a cross-cutting concern, not part of your core logic.

Instead of embedding retry logic inside your function, you declare it outside:

from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt

@retry(stop=stop_after_attempt(3))
def api_call(session, url, params=None, timeout_s=3.0):
    response = session.get(url, params=params, timeout=timeout_s)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response

The function code stays clean. The retry behaviour is explicit and separate.

docs.title: Making retry configuration explicit
docs.text:
I didn't want retry settings hardcoded in the decorator. They should live in configuration, just like API keys and timeouts.

I extended my Settings class:

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    # ...existing fields...
    retry_initial_wait_seconds: int = Field(default=1)
    max_retry_attempts: int = Field(default=3, ge=0, le=10)
    retry_backoff_multiplier: float = Field(default=2.0, gt=0)
    retry_max_wait_seconds: int = Field(default=60, gt=0)

Now retry behaviour is: - Validated (attempts must be between 0-10) - Documented (clear defaults and constraints) - Configurable (can be changed via environment variables)

docs.title: Implementing exponential backoff with jitter
docs.text:
Simple retries can cause thundering herd problems—if 1000 clients all retry at the same time, they create another spike of load.

The solution: exponential backoff with jitter.

from tenacity import wait_exponential_jitter

@retry(
    stop=stop_after_attempt(settings.max_retry_attempts),
    wait=wait_exponential_jitter(
        initial=settings.retry_initial_wait_seconds,
        max=settings.retry_max_wait_seconds,
        exp_base=settings.retry_backoff_multiplier,
    ),
    reraise=True,
)
def api_call(...):
    ...

This means: - First retry: wait ~1 second (+ random jitter) - Second retry: wait ~2 seconds (+ jitter) - Third retry: wait ~4 seconds (+ jitter) - Max wait: capped at 60 seconds

The jitter spreads out retry attempts, preventing synchronized retry storms.

docs.title: Only retrying retriable errors
docs.text:
Not all errors should trigger retries.

If I send a 401 Unauthorized, retrying won't help—my API key is wrong. But a 503 Service Unavailable is temporary.

I created a decision function:

def _should_retry(exception: BaseException) -> bool:
    """Determine if an exception should trigger a retry."""
    # Always retry network issues
    if isinstance(exception, (requests.Timeout, requests.ConnectionError)):
        return True

    # Only retry specific HTTP error codes
    if isinstance(exception, requests.HTTPError):
        if exception.response is not None:
            return exception.response.status_code in {408, 429, 500, 502, 503, 504}

    return False

This distinguishes between: - Transient failures (retry): timeouts, rate limits, server errors - Permanent failures (don't retry): authentication errors, not found, bad requests

docs.title: Adding observability with logging
docs.text:
When retries happen in production, I want to know about it.

tenacity provides callbacks for retry events:

from tenacity import before_sleep_log, after_log
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@retry(
    # ...retry config...
    before_sleep=before_sleep_log(logger, logging.WARNING),
    after=after_log(logger, logging.INFO),
    reraise=True,
)
def api_call(...):
    ...

Now when a retry happens: - Before sleeping: log at WARNING level (something failed) - After retry: log at INFO level (retry completed) - On final failure: the original exception is re-raised

This gives me visibility without cluttering my function code.

docs.title: The complete implementation
docs.text:
Here's the final api_call() function:

@retry(
    stop=stop_after_attempt(settings.max_retry_attempts),
    wait=wait_exponential_jitter(
        initial=settings.retry_initial_wait_seconds,
        max=settings.retry_max_wait_seconds,
        exp_base=settings.retry_backoff_multiplier,
    ),
    retry=retry_if_exception(_should_retry),
    before_sleep=before_sleep_log(logger, logging.WARNING),
    after=after_log(logger, logging.INFO),
    reraise=True,
)
def api_call(
    session: Session,
    url: str,
    params: dict[str, str | int | float] | None = None,
    timeout_s: float = 3.0,
) -> Response:
    """Make an HTTP GET request with retry logic."""
    response = session.get(url, params=params, timeout=timeout_s)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response

The core logic is unchanged—three lines of code.

All the retry behaviour is declarative and separate.

docs.title: What I learned
docs.text: Adding retry logic taught me several important lessons:

docs.title: 1. Resilience is a design concern, not an afterthought
docs.text: Retries shouldn't be bolted on later—they should be part of your architecture from the start.

docs.title: 2. Declarative is better than imperative
docs.text: Using decorators kept retry logic separate from business logic, making both easier to understand.

docs.title: 3. Configuration belongs in configuration
docs.text: Retry settings are just as important as API keys—they should be validated, documented, and configurable.

docs.title: 4. Not all failures are equal
docs.text: Distinguishing between retriable and non-retriable errors prevents wasted retries and makes debugging easier.

docs.title: 5. Observability matters
docs.text: Logging retry attempts gives you visibility into system health without requiring manual investigation.

docs.title: Comparing approaches
docs.text:
Approach Pros Cons Manual retry loop Full control, no dependencies Error-prone, hard to test, not reusable tenacity decorator Clean, declarative, well-tested Additional dependency Built-in urllib3 retries No extra library Limited to specific HTTP libraries

For my use case, tenacity was the clear winner—it separated concerns cleanly and made retry behaviour explicit and configurable.

docs.title: What's next
docs.text:
The retry logic is now solid, but there are still improvements to consider:

  • Circuit breaker pattern: stop retrying if the service is consistently down
  • Metrics collection: track retry rates and success rates
  • Adaptive backoff: adjust retry timing based on API response headers

But those can wait.

For now, I have a resilient, observable HTTP client—and that was the goal.

docs.title: Key takeaways
docs.text:
• Use libraries like tenacity for retry logic—don't roll your own
  • Make retry configuration explicit and validated
  • Use exponential backoff with jitter to prevent thundering herds
  • Only retry errors that are actually retriable
  • Add logging to make retry behaviour observable
  • Keep retry logic separate from business logic

Even simple API clients benefit from thoughtful resilience design.

docs.title: Designing a Simple, Testable Weather API Client in Python
docs.text:
When I started building a small Python client to fetch data from the OpenWeather API, my goal wasn’t to build a full pipeline or production system. It was to understand the design decisions that make Python code easier to reason about, test, and evolve.

This post walks through my thought process and the key choices I made while implementing a simple weather client.

docs.title: The problem I was solving
docs.text:
At its core, the task was simple:

  • Call an HTTP API
  • Handle success and failure correctly
  • Return parsed JSON data

However, even simple problems can turn messy quickly if responsibilities aren’t clear. I wanted to avoid:

  • hard-coded configuration
  • tightly coupled code
  • untestable HTTP logic

So I treated this as a design exercise, not just a coding task.

docs.title: Separating configuration from behaviour
docs.text:
One of the first decisions I made was to separate configuration from code logic.

Things like:

  • API keys
  • base URLs
  • timeouts

are not behaviour — they’re environment-specific configuration. Embedding them directly in the client would make the code harder to reuse and harder to test.

docs.title: Using a dedicated config module
docs.text:
I created a config.py file responsible solely for configuration:

class Settings(BaseSettings):
    openweather_api_key: str
    openweather_timeout_s: float = 10.0
    openweather_base_url: HttpUrl = "https://api.openweathermap.org/data/3.0/onecall/timemachine"

This immediately gave me:

  • a single source of truth for config
  • clear documentation of required vs optional settings
  • fast failure if configuration is invalid

docs.title: Why I used Pydantic for configuration
docs.text:
Rather than reading environment variables manually with os.environ, I used Pydantic Settings.

This was a deliberate choice.

docs.title: Benefits I gained immediately
docs.text:
• Validation at startup (e.g. timeout must be > 0)
  • Type safety (URLs must be valid URLs)
  • Cleaner code (no scattered os.environ lookups)
  • Clear failure modes (misconfiguration fails fast)

This pushed configuration errors to the edges of the system, where they belong.

docs.title: Designing the weather client around behaviour
docs.text:
The actual weather client function is intentionally small:

def fetch_openweather_data(*, session, url, params, timeout_s):
    response = session.get(url, params=params, timeout=timeout_s)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

This function does exactly three things:

  1. makes a request
  2. fails if the HTTP status is bad
  3. returns parsed JSON

No configuration. No retries. No orchestration.

Just behaviour.

docs.title: Dependency injection: passing in the session
docs.text:
One of the most important decisions I made was not to create the HTTP session inside the client.

Instead, I inject it:

fetch_openweather_data(session=session, ...)

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text:
By injecting the session:

  • the client has no hidden dependencies
  • HTTP configuration (headers, adapters, retries) lives elsewhere
  • the function is easy to test in isolation

The client doesn’t care where the session came from — only that it behaves like one.

This follows a simple rule:

Functions should depend on what they need, not where it comes from.

docs.title: How this enables easy testing
docs.text:
Because the session is injected, I can test the client without making real HTTP calls.

In tests, I pass in a mock session that:

  • pretends to have a .get() method
  • returns a fake response object
  • simulates success or failure

This allowed me to test:

  • success paths
  • error propagation
  • correct wiring of arguments

All without touching the network.

The design made testing straightforward — not something I had to “fight”.

docs.title: Freezing the scope at an MVP
docs.text:
During development, it became obvious how quickly this could grow:

  • retries with backoff
  • multiple locations and dates
  • partitioned storage
  • orchestration via Airflow

All valid ideas — but not necessary yet.

I deliberately froze the scope at a minimum viable client so I could:

  • reason clearly about the code
  • validate design choices through testing
  • avoid premature abstraction

This checkpoint now gives me a solid foundation to build on later.

docs.title: Key takeaways
docs.text:
What this small exercise reinforced for me:

  • Configuration is not behaviour — keep it separate
  • Validate configuration early and explicitly
  • Inject dependencies to keep code testable
  • Small, focused functions are easier to reason about
  • Stopping early is a design decision, not a failure

Even a simple API client benefits from thoughtful structure.

docs.title: What’s next
docs.text:
The next steps (when needed) are clear and incremental:

  • add retries with exponential backoff
  • extract parameter-building logic
  • integrate orchestration

But those decisions can wait.

For now, I have a clean, testable core — and that was the real goal.

docs.title: Docker Deep(ish) Dive
docs.text:
I recently did a Docker course on Udemy, but the real learning didn’t come from watching videos — it came from building, breaking, and rebuilding things myself.

What I thought Docker was and what it actually is turned out to be subtly but importantly different.

docs.title: Containers are not mini computers
docs.text:
The biggest mental shift for me was realising that a Docker container is not a lightweight VM.

A container: - does not have its own kernel - does not boot an operating system - is just one or more Linux processes, isolated by the kernel

On Linux, those processes run directly on the host kernel. On macOS and Windows, Docker Desktop quietly runs a single Linux VM, and all containers run as Linux processes inside that VM. The VM isn’t the point — it’s just there because macOS and Windows don’t have a Linux kernel.

That distinction matters.

docs.title: Images provide user-space, not an OS
docs.text:
When you write:

FROM python:3.12-slim

you’re not getting an operating system — you’re getting: - a Linux user-space filesystem - a Python binary - shared libraries Python depends on

The kernel always comes from the host (or the Docker Desktop VM). Containers share it.

That’s why containers start instantly and why you can run hundreds of them without “booting” anything.

docs.title: Containers have a filesystem — but it’s disposable
docs.text:
A container has a filesystem view built from: - read-only image layers - a thin writable layer on top

That writable layer disappears when the container is removed.

This explains a lot: - why logs “vanish” when a container exits - why batch jobs don’t hang around - why inspecting container filesystems is usually the wrong approach

If you care about data, you mount volumes.

Once I started mounting data/ and logs/ from the host, Docker stopped feeling mysterious and started feeling mechanical — in a good way.

docs.title: docker exec is not SSH
docs.text:
Another subtle but important insight: docker exec doesn’t connect over the network. It just asks the kernel to start another process inside the same container namespaces.

That’s why it’s instant, and why it only works when the container is running.

docs.title: Dockerfiles vs docker-compose
docs.text:
The distinction finally clicked:

  • Dockerfile: What is this thing? (base image, dependencies, code, default command)

  • docker-compose: How does it run? (env vars, volumes, ports, multiple containers)

Once I separated “build” from “run”, things stopped getting tangled.

docs.title: Why this is such a cool technology
docs.text:
Docker feels powerful not because it’s magical, but because it’s honest: - processes, not machines - explicit filesystem access - disposable by default - reproducible by design

It forces good habits — especially around stateless execution and externalised data — and once you internalise the model, a lot of modern infrastructure suddenly makes sense.

I went in expecting “containers”. I came out understanding processes, kernels, filesystems, and isolation a bit better.

That’s a pretty good trade.

docs.title: Starting the PyBites PDM – from “can code” to “can build”
docs.text:
I’ve started the PyBites Professional Developer Mindset (PDM) program, and it already feels like the right kind of discomfort.

Not the “I don’t understand Python at all” kind of discomfort — but the subtler, more interesting one:

realising there are gaps between writing Python that works and writing Python that holds up in production.

docs.title: Why I signed up
docs.text:
I can write Python. I’ve done it professionally.

I’ve built pipelines, Lambdas, Glue jobs, and scripts that solve real problems. I can usually get things working, debug issues when they arise, and ship solutions that deliver value.

But I kept noticing something.

  • I could get things working
  • I could debug issues
  • I could ship solutions

Yet when I read really good Python — the kind that’s calm, intentional, and obvious — I felt a gap.

Not a knowledge gap exactly. More a mindset gap.

That’s what drew me to PDM.

docs.title: What “developer mindset” actually means (so far)
docs.text:
Very early on, one thing became clear: this isn’t about learning new syntax.

It’s about learning to consistently ask better questions:

  • What is the responsibility of this piece of code?
  • What assumptions am I making here?
  • Could someone else understand this without me explaining it?
  • Would I be happy maintaining this in six months?

None of those questions are Python-specific — but they show up in Python, every day.

docs.title: The first small but important shift
docs.text:
One change I’ve already made as a direct result of starting PDM is this:

I’m writing notes as if I’m teaching.

Not documentation-after-the-fact, but thinking-through-the-problem-on-paper:

  • Why did this fail?
  • What did I misunderstand?
  • What’s the underlying mechanism?
  • What’s the rule of thumb I want to remember?

This blog exists largely for that reason.

Writing forces me to be precise. Precision exposes gaps. Gaps tell me what to work on next.

docs.title: What I’m hoping to get out of PDM
docs.text:
I’m not chasing “clever” code.

I want to get better at:

  • Writing simple code that stays simple
  • Making intent obvious
  • Reducing cognitive load
  • Being deliberate about structure, not accidental
  • Developing habits that scale beyond a single script

In short: becoming someone who builds systems, not just solutions.

docs.title: Early takeaway
docs.text:
If I had to summarise the early feeling in one sentence:

This isn’t about learning more Python — it’s about learning how to think while writing Python.

That feels like time well spent.

I’ll keep using this blog to capture what I’m learning, where I’m getting stuck, and what’s starting to click.

docs.title: Understanding the Repository Pattern: Separating Data Access from Business Logic
docs.text:
After learning about abstract base classes, I wanted to see them used in a real design pattern. The repository pattern was the perfect next step.

The idea is simple: your business logic shouldn't know or care where the data comes from. It just asks a repository for it.

This post explains how I implemented the repository pattern using weather data, with swappable pandas and polars backends.

docs.title: The problem: business logic tied to data access
docs.text:
Imagine I have a function that reads weather data from a JSON file and calculates the average temperature:

import json
import pandas as pd

def weather_summary():
    with open("weather_data.json") as f:
        raw = json.load(f)

    df = pd.DataFrame(raw["data"])
    avg_temp = df["temp"].mean()
    print(f"Average temperature: {avg_temp:.1f}°C")

This works—but it has problems:

  • Tightly coupled: the function depends directly on pandas and the file format
  • Hard to test: you can't test the summary logic without a real JSON file
  • Hard to change: switching to polars means rewriting the whole function
  • Mixed responsibilities: data loading and business logic are tangled together

docs.title: The repository pattern: a middleman for your data
docs.text:
The repository pattern introduces a layer between your business logic and your data source.

Think of it like ordering food at a restaurant:

  • You (the business logic) ask the waiter (the repository) for food
  • You don't care whether the kitchen uses a gas oven or an electric one
  • You just get your meal in a consistent format

In code terms:

  • Your business logic asks a repository for data
  • The repository returns it in a consistent format (dataclasses)
  • You don't care whether it used pandas, polars, SQL, or an API behind the scenes

docs.title: Step 1: define the data model
docs.text:
First, I needed a simple data container for a weather record. This has no dependencies on any library—it's just a plain dataclass:

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class WeatherRecord:
    dt: int             # Unix timestamp
    temp: float         # Temperature in Celsius
    humidity: int       # Humidity percentage
    description: str    # e.g., "overcast clouds"

Using frozen=True makes the records immutable—once created, they can't be changed. This prevents accidental data corruption downstream.

docs.title: Step 2: define the repository interface
docs.text:
Next, I defined the contract that any repository must follow. This uses an abstract base class:

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class WeatherRepository(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def get_weather(self) -> list[WeatherRecord]:
        """Return a list of WeatherRecords from some data source."""
        ...

This says: "Any class that inherits from WeatherRepository must implement a get_weather() method that returns a list of WeatherRecord objects."

It doesn't say how to get the data. That's the whole point.

docs.title: Step 3: implement a pandas repository
docs.text:
The first concrete implementation uses pandas to load and parse the JSON data:

import json
import pandas as pd

class PandasWeatherRepository(WeatherRepository):
    def __init__(self, filepath: str):
        self.filepath = filepath

    def get_weather(self) -> list[WeatherRecord]:
        with open(self.filepath) as f:
            raw = json.load(f)

        df = pd.DataFrame(raw["data"])

        records = []
        for _, row in df.iterrows():
            records.append(
                WeatherRecord(
                    dt=row["dt"],
                    temp=row["temp"],
                    humidity=row["humidity"],
                    description=row["weather"][0]["description"],
                )
            )
        return records

The key detail: the nested "weather" field in the JSON contains a list of dictionaries. We extract the description with row["weather"][0]["description"].

docs.title: Step 4: implement a polars repository
docs.text:
The second implementation does the same thing using polars:

import json
import polars as pl

class PolarsWeatherRepository(WeatherRepository):
    def __init__(self, filepath: str):
        self.filepath = filepath

    def get_weather(self) -> list[WeatherRecord]:
        with open(self.filepath) as f:
            raw = json.load(f)

        # Extract descriptions before creating the DataFrame
        for entry in raw["data"]:
            entry["description"] = entry["weather"][0]["description"]

        df = pl.DataFrame(raw["data"])

        records = []
        for row in df.iter_rows(named=True):
            records.append(
                WeatherRecord(
                    dt=row["dt"],
                    temp=row["temp"],
                    humidity=row["humidity"],
                    description=row["description"],
                )
            )
        return records

Polars doesn't handle nested data as easily as pandas, so I extracted the description before creating the DataFrame. The end result is identical—a list of WeatherRecord objects.

docs.title: Step 5: write business logic that uses the interface
docs.text:
Now the important part—business logic that only depends on the interface:

def weather_summary(repo: WeatherRepository) -> None:
    records = repo.get_weather()

    avg_temp = sum(r.temp for r in records) / len(records)
    avg_humidity = sum(r.humidity for r in records) / len(records)

    print(f"Total records: {len(records)}")
    print(f"Average temperature: {avg_temp:.1f}°C")
    print(f"Average humidity: {avg_humidity:.1f}%")
    for r in records:
        print(f"  {r.dt} | {r.temp}°C | {r.humidity}% | {r.description}")

Notice what this function doesn't import: no pandas, no polars, no json. It only knows about WeatherRepository and WeatherRecord.

docs.title: Swapping repositories without changing business logic
docs.text:
Here's where the pattern pays off:

if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Using pandas
    pandas_repo = PandasWeatherRepository("weather_data.json")
    print("=== Pandas Repository ===")
    weather_summary(pandas_repo)

    # Using polars
    polars_repo = PolarsWeatherRepository("weather_data.json")
    print("\n=== Polars Repository ===")
    weather_summary(polars_repo)

Both produce exactly the same output. The weather_summary() function didn't change at all. I just swapped the repository.

docs.title: Project structure
docs.text:
In a real project, each piece would live in its own file:

weather_project/
├── models.py                  # WeatherRecord dataclass
├── repository.py              # WeatherRepository ABC (interface)
├── pandas_repository.py       # PandasWeatherRepository
├── polars_repository.py       # PolarsWeatherRepository
├── weather_summary.py         # Business logic
├── main.py                    # Entry point, picks which repo to use
└── weather_data.json          # Data file

This separation keeps things clean:

  • Someone using polars doesn't need pandas installed (and vice versa)
  • Each file has a single responsibility
  • Adding a new repository (e.g., sql_repository.py) doesn't touch any existing files

docs.title: What I learned
docs.text: Building this taught me several important lessons:

docs.title: 1. Interfaces create flexibility
docs.text: By defining WeatherRepository as an abstract class, I created a contract that any data source can fulfil. The business logic doesn't care about the implementation details.

docs.title: 2. Dataclasses are the glue
docs.text: WeatherRecord acts as a common language between the repositories and the business logic. Everyone agrees on the shape of the data.

docs.title: 3. Separation of concerns matters
docs.text: Keeping data access separate from business logic makes both easier to understand, test, and change independently.

docs.title: 4. The pattern makes testing easy
docs.text:
I could create an InMemoryWeatherRepository for testing that returns hardcoded data—no files, no libraries, no network calls needed:

class InMemoryWeatherRepository(WeatherRepository):
    def __init__(self, records: list[WeatherRecord]):
        self._records = records

    def get_weather(self) -> list[WeatherRecord]:
        return self._records

docs.title: 5. ABCs vs Protocols
docs.text: Python offers two ways to define interfaces—ABCs (used here) and Protocols. ABCs enforce the contract at class definition time through inheritance. Protocols use structural typing (duck typing)—any class with the right methods qualifies, without needing to inherit anything.

docs.title: Comparing approaches
docs.text:
Approach Pros Cons Everything in one function Simple, quick to write Tightly coupled, hard to test or change Repository with ABC Enforced contract, clear inheritance Requires subclassing Repository with Protocol No inheritance needed, duck typing Contract only checked by type checkers

For learning purposes, the ABC approach made the pattern most explicit and visible. In production, either approach works well.

docs.title: Key takeaways
docs.text:
• The repository pattern separates what data you need from how you get it
  • Define a clear interface using an ABC or Protocol
  • Use dataclasses as a common data format between layers
  • Business logic should depend on interfaces, not implementations
  • This makes your code flexible, testable, and easy to extend
  • Each repository can live in its own file with its own dependencies

Even for a simple weather data project, the repository pattern made the code cleaner and more maintainable. It's a pattern I'll reach for again.

docs.title: Setting Up a Production-Grade Python Project (From Scratch)
docs.text:
Recently, I deliberately slowed down and spent time setting up a production-grade Python project from first principles, rather than jumping straight into feature development. The goal wasn’t to “get something working”, but to understand how the tooling fits together and why these practices matter in real teams.

This post captures what I focused on and what I learned.

docs.title: Why start from scratch?
docs.text:
It’s very easy to inherit a project where: - CI already exists - pre-commit hooks are already installed - pyproject.toml is already configured

The problem is that you can use these things without really understanding them.

I wanted to: - understand when and why checks run - know what lives locally vs in CI - be confident adjusting or introducing standards in the future - remove “cargo-cult” tooling from my workflow

docs.title: A single source of truth: pyproject.toml
docs.text:
I started by treating pyproject.toml as the central contract for the project.

Key things I paid attention to: - Separating runtime dependencies from development dependencies - Explicitly configuring tooling rather than relying on defaults - Making sure linting, formatting, typing, and testing all had a clear home

This helped reinforce that: - tooling configuration belongs with the code - new contributors should get the same behaviour by default - CI should simply reuse what’s already defined locally

docs.title: Ruff, pytest, and mypy: clear responsibilities
docs.text:
I made sure I was clear on what each tool is responsible for:

  • Ruff Linting and formatting — fast feedback, consistent style, fewer bikeshedding discussions.

  • pytest Executable specifications. Tests describe what the code should do, not how it does it.

  • mypy Static type checking as a design aid, not a runtime requirement. Python still runs dynamically, but mypy gives early signals when assumptions don’t line up.

Understanding these roles made the setup feel intentional rather than “tool soup”.

docs.title: Pre-commit hooks: shifting quality left
docs.text:
One of the most useful things I revisited was pre-commit.

What clicked for me this time: - Pre-commit is a gate, not a suggestion - Hooks failing means the commit does not happen - Auto-fixing hooks deliberately fail so you re-stage changes consciously

This creates a workflow where: - mistakes are caught before they enter history - CI becomes less noisy - “fix lint” commits disappear

Local correctness first, shared correctness later.

docs.title: GitHub Actions: CI as an extension of local checks
docs.text:
Rather than inventing new rules in CI, I mirrored the local checks:

  • lint
  • format check
  • type check
  • tests + coverage

The important realisation here was:

CI should verify standards, not define them.

If something fails in CI but wasn’t enforced locally, that’s a design smell.

docs.title: Why this mattered before writing more code
docs.text:
At first glance, this kind of setup can feel like “not making progress”.

In reality: - I now have a repeatable baseline for future projects - I’m confident explaining and justifying these choices to others - adding new code feels safer and more deliberate - I’m better equipped to improve or introduce standards in existing codebases

Most importantly, the tooling now supports learning rather than getting in the way of it.

docs.title: Final reflection
docs.text:
This was a reminder that good engineering isn’t just about writing code — it’s about creating an environment where good code is the default outcome.

Taking the time to understand the foundations has already paid off, and it’s a pattern I plan to repeat whenever I start something new.

docs.title: The value of a mentor when starting a new job
docs.text:
Starting a new technical role always comes with a certain amount of uncertainty.

New systems. New people. New expectations.

Even when you know you’re capable, there’s usually a period where it feels like you’re operating slightly outside your comfort zone.

For me, the early weeks of my new role had exactly that feeling.

What made a huge difference was having a mentor to talk things through with.

docs.title: The early phase: imposter syndrome
docs.text:
The first few days in a new job can be strange.

You suddenly find yourself surrounded by:

  • unfamiliar tools
  • unfamiliar codebases
  • unfamiliar architecture

Even if you’ve done similar work before, there’s an inevitable moment where you think:

“Maybe I’m out of my depth here.”

Logically, you know that’s unlikely. But emotionally, it’s still easy to feel that way when everything around you is new.

Having a mentor during that phase helped enormously.

Not because they solved the problems for me, but because they helped normalise the experience.

The message was simple:

Everyone goes through this phase when starting somewhere new.

Just hearing that from someone experienced takes a surprising amount of pressure away.

docs.title: A sounding board for ideas
docs.text:
One of the most valuable parts of mentoring isn’t instruction, it’s conversation.

Being able to talk through problems, ideas, or uncertainties with someone outside your immediate work environment changes the dynamic completely.

Instead of thinking:

“I need to figure this out perfectly before I say anything.”

You can say:

“Here’s what I’m seeing. Does this make sense?”

That small shift makes it much easier to reason through technical or architectural questions.

Often the answer emerges just from explaining the situation clearly.

docs.title: A practical framework for working
docs.text:
Another big benefit was having a clearer framework for how to approach development work.

Through the PyBites PDM programme I was introduced to ideas such as:

  • structuring Python projects properly
  • using modern tooling like uv, ruff, and pre-commit
  • working with pull requests and clean development workflows
  • thinking deliberately about project structure

None of these things are individually revolutionary.

But together they create a way of working.

That framework gave me a solid starting point when tackling problems at work, rather than feeling like I was improvising everything from scratch.

docs.title: Confidence to contribute
docs.text:
One interesting side effect of that framework was increased confidence.

Because I had exposure to practices such as:

  • automated testing
  • code quality checks
  • pre-commit hooks
  • structured project layouts

I felt comfortable suggesting ideas within my team that hadn’t been widely adopted yet.

Not because I was certain they were perfect solutions, but because I had enough understanding to contribute to the discussion.

That’s a subtle but important shift.

Instead of feeling like the new person trying to catch up, you start feeling like someone who can add value.

docs.title: Architectural thinking
docs.text:
Another unexpected benefit was exposure to higher-level design ideas.

Concepts such as:

  • repository patterns
  • abstract base classes
  • clearer separation of responsibilities

started to change how I looked at problems.

Sometimes you recognise patterns that you previously would have solved in a much more ad-hoc way.

Other times you simply become more deliberate about design decisions.

Either way, it pushes you toward writing code that is easier to understand and maintain.

docs.title: The real value: a roadmap
docs.text:
If I had to summarise the biggest benefit of mentoring in one phrase, it would be this:

It gave me a roadmap.

Not a rigid set of instructions, but a sense of direction.

Instead of trying to learn everything randomly, there was a clearer idea of:

  • what good engineering practices look like
  • which tools are worth investing in
  • how experienced developers approach problems

That makes the early weeks of a new role much easier to navigate.

docs.title: Final reflection
docs.text:
A mentor doesn’t remove the need to do the work yourself.

You still have to:

  • learn the systems
  • investigate problems
  • write the code
  • make the mistakes

But having someone experienced to talk things through with can accelerate that process dramatically.

It reduces uncertainty, reinforces good practices, and helps you build confidence faster.

For me, it felt a bit like having a map at the start of a journey.

You still have to walk the path, but you’re far less likely to wander in circles.
